questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the nuclear model of the atom.

questions.skill: Describe the nuclear model of the atom.
questions.stem: Which statement describes the structure of an atom?
questions.question: Which statement describes the structure of an atom?
options: A positive nucleus surrounded by electrons.
A negative nucleus surrounded by protons.
Electrons and neutrons only, with no nucleus.
A solid sphere with no smaller particles.
questions.correctAnswer: A positive nucleus surrounded by electrons.
questions.explanation: The nuclear model describes an atom as a small positive nucleus surrounded by electrons.

distractorRationales.text: A negative nucleus surrounded by protons.
Electrons and neutrons only, with no nucleus.
A solid sphere with no smaller particles.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the nuclear model of the atom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the relative charge of subatomic particles.

questions.skill: Recall the relative charge of subatomic particles.
questions.stem: Which particle has a relative charge of -1?
questions.question: Which particle has a relative charge of -1?
options: Electron
Neutron
Proton
Alpha particle
questions.correctAnswer: Electron
questions.explanation: An electron has relative charge -1. A proton has +1 and a neutron has 0.

distractorRationales.text: Neutron
Proton
Alpha particle

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the relative charge of subatomic particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate mass number from protons and neutrons.

questions.skill: Calculate mass number from protons and neutrons.
questions.stem: An atom has 9 protons and 10 neutrons. What is its mass number?
questions.question: An atom has 9 protons and 10 neutrons. What is its mass number?
questions.explanation: Mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons, so 9 + 10 = 19.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate mass number from protons and neutrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.

learningObjective.statement: Find electron number for a neutral atom.

questions.skill: Find electron number for a neutral atom.
questions.stem: A neutral atom has 13 protons. How many electrons does it have?
questions.question: A neutral atom has 13 protons. How many electrons does it have?
questions.explanation: In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Find electron number for a neutral atom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.
questions.stem: Two atoms are isotopes of the same element. Which statement must be true?
questions.question: Two atoms are isotopes of the same element. Which statement must be true?
options: They have different numbers of protons.
They have the same mass number.
They have the same number of protons.
They have no neutrons.
questions.correctAnswer: They have the same number of protons.
questions.explanation: Isotopes of the same element have the same proton number but different numbers of neutrons.

distractorRationales.text: They have different numbers of protons.
They have the same mass number.
They have no neutrons.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe isotopes using proton number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.
questions.stem: An atom forms a positive ion. Which change has happened?
questions.question: An atom forms a positive ion. Which change has happened?
options: It has lost electrons.
It has gained electrons.
It has gained neutrons.
It has lost protons.
questions.correctAnswer: It has lost electrons.
questions.explanation: A positive ion forms when an atom loses electrons, leaving more protons than electrons.

distractorRationales.text: It has gained electrons.
It has gained neutrons.
It has lost protons.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how positive ions form.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain what is meant by background radiation.

learningObjective.statement: Identify natural sources of background radiation.

questions.skill: Identify natural sources of background radiation.
questions.stem: Which is a natural source of background radiation?
questions.question: Which is a natural source of background radiation?
options: Cosmic rays from space.
A loudspeaker.
A filament lamp.
A low-voltage battery.
questions.correctAnswer: Cosmic rays from space.
questions.explanation: Cosmic rays are a natural source of background radiation.

distractorRationales.text: A loudspeaker.
A filament lamp.
A low-voltage battery.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.12.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify natural sources of background radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain what is meant by background radiation.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why background radiation varies by location.

questions.skill: Explain why background radiation varies by location.
questions.stem: A house is built in an area with granite rocks. Why might background radiation be higher there?
questions.question: A house is built in an area with granite rocks. Why might background radiation be higher there?
options: Some rocks contain radioactive materials.
Granite blocks all radiation from space.
Rocks create microwaves when heated.
All houses become radioactive over time.
questions.correctAnswer: Some rocks contain radioactive materials.
questions.explanation: Some rocks contain radioactive materials that add to background radiation.

distractorRationales.text: Granite blocks all radiation from space.
Rocks create microwaves when heated.
All houses become radioactive over time.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.12.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why background radiation varies by location.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain what is meant by background radiation.

learningObjective.statement: Subtract background radiation from a measured count rate.

questions.skill: Subtract background radiation from a measured count rate.
questions.stem: A detector records 64 counts per minute near a source. The background count is 18 counts per minute. What is the corrected count rate for the source?
questions.question: A detector records 64 counts per minute near a source. The background count is 18 counts per minute. What is the corrected count rate for the source?
options: 18 counts per minute
46 counts per minute
64 counts per minute
82 counts per minute
questions.correctAnswer: 46 counts per minute
questions.explanation: The corrected count rate is the measured count rate minus background: 64 - 18 = 46 counts per minute.

distractorRationales.text: 18 counts per minute
64 counts per minute
82 counts per minute

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.12.
questions.senNote: Checks: Subtract background radiation from a measured count rate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain what is meant by background radiation.
questions.stem: Why is background radiation not exactly the same everywhere?
questions.question: Why is background radiation not exactly the same everywhere?
options: Different places have different natural and human-made sources.
Radiation only exists inside hospitals.
Background radiation stops at night.
All detectors give the same reading in every place.
questions.correctAnswer: Different places have different natural and human-made sources.
questions.explanation: Background radiation varies because sources such as rocks, cosmic rays and medical exposure vary.

distractorRationales.text: Radiation only exists inside hospitals.
Background radiation stops at night.
All detectors give the same reading in every place.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.12.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe why background radiation varies.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain what is meant by background radiation.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why background count is measured.

questions.skill: Explain why background count is measured.
questions.stem: Why should background radiation be measured before testing a radioactive source?
questions.question: Why should background radiation be measured before testing a radioactive source?
options: So it can be subtracted from the measured count rate.
So it can be added twice to the source count rate.
So the detector stops detecting radiation.
So the source becomes less radioactive.
questions.correctAnswer: So it can be subtracted from the measured count rate.
questions.explanation: Background radiation contributes to the detector reading, so it should be subtracted to estimate the source count rate.

distractorRationales.text: So it can be added twice to the source count rate.
So the detector stops detecting radiation.
So the source becomes less radioactive.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.12.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why background count is measured.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe nuclear fission and fusion.
questions.stem: What happens during nuclear fission?
questions.question: What happens during nuclear fission?
options: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei.
Two small nuclei join to form a larger nucleus.
An electron moves to a higher shell.
A solid changes into a liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei.
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission is the splitting of a large unstable nucleus into smaller nuclei, releasing energy.

distractorRationales.text: Two small nuclei join to form a larger nucleus.
An electron moves to a higher shell.
A solid changes into a liquid.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.35P.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe nuclear fission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe nuclear fission and fusion.

learningObjective.statement: State how fission can be triggered.

questions.skill: State how fission can be triggered.
questions.stem: In a nuclear reactor, what can trigger the fission of a uranium nucleus?
questions.question: In a nuclear reactor, what can trigger the fission of a uranium nucleus?
options: Absorption of a neutron.
Absorption of a proton.
Emission of visible light.
Cooling the nucleus to room temperature.
questions.correctAnswer: Absorption of a neutron.
questions.explanation: A uranium nucleus can undergo fission after absorbing a neutron.

distractorRationales.text: Absorption of a proton.
Emission of visible light.
Cooling the nucleus to room temperature.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.35P.
questions.senNote: Checks: State how fission can be triggered.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe nuclear fission and fusion.
questions.stem: Why can nuclear fission lead to a chain reaction?
questions.question: Why can nuclear fission lead to a chain reaction?
options: Each fission event can release neutrons that cause further fission.
Each fission event removes all neutrons from the reactor.
Gamma rays always turn into protons.
The fuel rods become magnets.
questions.correctAnswer: Each fission event can release neutrons that cause further fission.
questions.explanation: Fission releases neutrons. These neutrons can be absorbed by other nuclei and trigger more fission events.

distractorRationales.text: Each fission event removes all neutrons from the reactor.
Gamma rays always turn into protons.
The fuel rods become magnets.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.35P.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain a fission chain reaction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe nuclear fission and fusion.
questions.stem: What happens during nuclear fusion?
questions.question: What happens during nuclear fusion?
options: Two light nuclei join to form a heavier nucleus.
A large nucleus splits into two smaller nuclei.
Electrons are shared between atoms.
A neutron changes into an electron only.
questions.correctAnswer: Two light nuclei join to form a heavier nucleus.
questions.explanation: Fusion is the joining of light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy.

distractorRationales.text: A large nucleus splits into two smaller nuclei.
Electrons are shared between atoms.
A neutron changes into an electron only.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.35P.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe nuclear fusion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe nuclear fission and fusion.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how control rods affect a chain reaction.

questions.skill: Explain how control rods affect a chain reaction.
questions.stem: What is the purpose of control rods in a nuclear reactor?
questions.question: What is the purpose of control rods in a nuclear reactor?
options: To absorb neutrons.
To add extra uranium nuclei.
To turn gamma rays into alpha particles.
To increase the temperature of the coolant directly.
questions.correctAnswer: To absorb neutrons.
questions.explanation: Control rods absorb neutrons, reducing the number available to cause further fission events.

distractorRationales.text: To add extra uranium nuclei.
To turn gamma rays into alpha particles.
To increase the temperature of the coolant directly.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.35P.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how control rods affect a chain reaction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain half-life and use it in simple calculations.
questions.stem: What is meant by the half-life of a radioactive isotope?
questions.question: What is meant by the half-life of a radioactive isotope?
options: The time taken for the activity to fall to half its original value.
The time taken for all nuclei in the sample to decay.
The time taken for the activity to double.
Half the age of the isotope.
questions.correctAnswer: The time taken for the activity to fall to half its original value.
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity, or number of undecayed nuclei, to fall to half its original value.

distractorRationales.text: The time taken for all nuclei in the sample to decay.
The time taken for the activity to double.
Half the age of the isotope.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.25.
questions.senNote: Checks: Define half-life.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain half-life and use it in simple calculations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate activity after whole numbers of half-lives.

questions.skill: Calculate activity after whole numbers of half-lives.
questions.stem: A sample has an activity of 800 Bq. Its half-life is 5 minutes. What is its activity after 10 minutes?
questions.question: A sample has an activity of 800 Bq. Its half-life is 5 minutes. What is its activity after 10 minutes?
options: 200 Bq
400 Bq
790 Bq
questions.correctAnswer: 200 Bq
questions.explanation: 10 minutes is two half-lives. The activity halves from 800 Bq to 400 Bq, then to 200 Bq.

distractorRationales.text: 400 Bq
790 Bq

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.25.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate activity after whole numbers of half-lives.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain half-life and use it in simple calculations.
questions.stem: On a decay graph, the count rate falls from 120 counts per second to 60 counts per second in 8 minutes. What is the half-life?
questions.question: On a decay graph, the count rate falls from 120 counts per second to 60 counts per second in 8 minutes. What is the half-life?
options: 4 minutes
8 minutes
60 minutes
120 minutes
questions.correctAnswer: 8 minutes
questions.explanation: The half-life is the time for the count rate to halve. It halves from 120 to 60 in 8 minutes.

distractorRationales.text: 4 minutes
60 minutes
120 minutes

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.25.
questions.senNote: Checks: Find half-life from count-rate data.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain half-life and use it in simple calculations.

learningObjective.statement: Link random decay to half-life patterns.

questions.skill: Link random decay to half-life patterns.
questions.stem: Which statement links half-life and radioactive decay correctly?
questions.question: Which statement links half-life and radioactive decay correctly?
options: A single nucleus decays at a predictable time.
A large sample shows a predictable half-life pattern.
Half-life means no nuclei decay randomly.
All nuclei decay exactly at the half-life time.
questions.correctAnswer: A large sample shows a predictable half-life pattern.
questions.explanation: Decay of a single nucleus is random, but a large sample shows a predictable half-life pattern.

distractorRationales.text: A single nucleus decays at a predictable time.
Half-life means no nuclei decay randomly.
All nuclei decay exactly at the half-life time.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.25.
questions.senNote: Checks: Link random decay to half-life patterns.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain half-life and use it in simple calculations.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a suitable half-life for a medical tracer.

questions.skill: Choose a suitable half-life for a medical tracer.
questions.stem: Why should a medical tracer not have a very long half-life?
questions.question: Why should a medical tracer not have a very long half-life?
options: It would remain active in the body for too long.
It would not emit any radiation.
It would become a stable isotope immediately.
It would stop detectors working.
questions.correctAnswer: It would remain active in the body for too long.
questions.explanation: A very long half-life would keep the tracer radioactive in the body for longer than needed, increasing dose.

distractorRationales.text: It would not emit any radiation.
It would become a stable isotope immediately.
It would stop detectors working.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.25.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a suitable half-life for a medical tracer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe dangers of ionising radiation and precautions.
questions.stem: What is irradiation?
questions.question: What is irradiation?
options: Being exposed to radiation from a source.
Having radioactive material on the skin.
Swallowing a radioactive substance.
Changing every atom into an isotope.
questions.correctAnswer: Being exposed to radiation from a source.
questions.explanation: Irradiation means exposure to radiation. Contamination means radioactive material is on or inside something.

distractorRationales.text: Having radioactive material on the skin.
Swallowing a radioactive substance.
Changing every atom into an isotope.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.29.
questions.senNote: Checks: Distinguish irradiation from contamination.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe dangers of ionising radiation and precautions.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a safety precaution for handling radioactive sources.

questions.skill: Choose a safety precaution for handling radioactive sources.
questions.stem: Which action reduces the risk from handling a radioactive source?
questions.question: Which action reduces the risk from handling a radioactive source?
options: Holding the source closer to the body.
Increasing the time spent near the source.
Using tongs to increase distance from the source.
Removing shielding from the source.
questions.correctAnswer: Using tongs to increase distance from the source.
questions.explanation: Increasing distance from the source reduces the dose received. Tongs help keep the source away from the body.

distractorRationales.text: Holding the source closer to the body.
Increasing the time spent near the source.
Removing shielding from the source.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.29.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a safety precaution for handling radioactive sources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe dangers of ionising radiation and precautions.

learningObjective.statement: Choose suitable shielding for gamma radiation.

questions.skill: Choose suitable shielding for gamma radiation.
questions.stem: Which material is most suitable for reducing exposure to gamma radiation?
questions.question: Which material is most suitable for reducing exposure to gamma radiation?
options: Paper
Air
Thin aluminium foil
Thick lead
questions.correctAnswer: Thick lead
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is very penetrating, so dense shielding such as thick lead is used to reduce exposure.

distractorRationales.text: Paper
Air
Thin aluminium foil

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.29.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose suitable shielding for gamma radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe dangers of ionising radiation and precautions.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the risk from internal alpha contamination.

questions.skill: Explain the risk from internal alpha contamination.
questions.stem: Why is alpha contamination inside the body dangerous?
questions.question: Why is alpha contamination inside the body dangerous?
options: Alpha radiation is strongly ionising and can damage nearby cells.
Alpha radiation passes through the body without ionising.
Alpha radiation is the least ionising type of nuclear radiation.
Alpha radiation can only damage cells outside the body.
questions.correctAnswer: Alpha radiation is strongly ionising and can damage nearby cells.
questions.explanation: Alpha radiation is strongly ionising. It is especially hazardous if the source is inside the body close to living cells.

distractorRationales.text: Alpha radiation passes through the body without ionising.
Alpha radiation is the least ionising type of nuclear radiation.
Alpha radiation can only damage cells outside the body.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 6.29.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the risk from internal alpha contamination.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate resistance using potential difference and current.

questions.skill: Calculate resistance using potential difference and current.
questions.stem: A resistor has a potential difference of 6.0 V across it and a current of 0.30 A through it. What is its resistance?
questions.question: A resistor has a potential difference of 6.0 V across it and a current of 0.30 A through it. What is its resistance?
options: 0.050 ohms
1.8 ohms
5.7 ohms
20 ohms
questions.correctAnswer: 20 ohms
questions.explanation: Use resistance = potential difference ÷ current. R = 6.0 ÷ 0.30 = 20 ohms.

distractorRationales.text: 0.050 ohms
1.8 ohms
5.7 ohms

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate resistance using potential difference and current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the I-V graph for an ohmic conductor.

questions.skill: Identify the I-V graph for an ohmic conductor.
questions.stem: Which graph would show an ohmic conductor at constant temperature?
questions.question: Which graph would show an ohmic conductor at constant temperature?
options: A straight line through the origin.
A curve that becomes flat as potential difference increases.
A line with current only in one direction.
A horizontal line at zero current.
questions.correctAnswer: A straight line through the origin.
questions.explanation: A straight line through the origin shows that current and potential difference increase in the same ratio.

distractorRationales.text: A curve that becomes flat as potential difference increases.
distractorRationales.misconception: filament lamp confused with ohmic conductor

distractorRationales.text: A line with current only in one direction.
distractorRationales.misconception: diode confused with ohmic conductor

distractorRationales.text: A horizontal line at zero current.
distractorRationales.misconception: open circuit confused with conductor behaviour

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the I-V graph for an ohmic conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Apply direct proportionality for an ohmic conductor.

questions.skill: Apply direct proportionality for an ohmic conductor.
questions.stem: A fixed resistor is kept at constant temperature. The potential difference across it is doubled. What happens to the current?
questions.question: A fixed resistor is kept at constant temperature. The potential difference across it is doubled. What happens to the current?
options: It doubles.
It halves.
It stays the same.
It becomes zero.
questions.correctAnswer: It doubles.
questions.explanation: For an ohmic conductor at constant temperature, current is directly proportional to potential difference.

distractorRationales.text: It halves.
distractorRationales.misconception: inverse relationship assumed

distractorRationales.text: It stays the same.
distractorRationales.misconception: resistance confused with current

distractorRationales.text: It becomes zero.
distractorRationales.misconception: larger potential difference confused with circuit break

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Apply direct proportionality for an ohmic conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the shape of a filament lamp I-V graph.

questions.skill: Explain the shape of a filament lamp I-V graph.
questions.stem: The I-V graph for a filament lamp curves as the potential difference increases. Why does the graph curve?
questions.question: The I-V graph for a filament lamp curves as the potential difference increases. Why does the graph curve?
options: The filament gets hotter, so its resistance increases.
The filament loses all resistance when it glows.
The current changes direction every second.
The cell runs out of charge at high potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: The filament gets hotter, so its resistance increases.
questions.explanation: As the filament heats up, the metal ions vibrate more and the resistance increases, so current rises less quickly.

distractorRationales.text: The filament loses all resistance when it glows.
distractorRationales.misconception: hot filament resistance decreases to zero

distractorRationales.text: The current changes direction every second.
distractorRationales.misconception: alternating current confused with lamp characteristic

distractorRationales.text: The cell runs out of charge at high potential difference.
distractorRationales.misconception: charge supply misconception

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the shape of a filament lamp I-V graph.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the current-voltage characteristic of a diode.

questions.skill: Describe the current-voltage characteristic of a diode.
questions.stem: Which statement about a diode is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about a diode is correct?
options: It allows current to flow much more easily in one direction than the other.
It has the same resistance in both directions.
It stores charge like a cell.
It changes alternating current into a magnetic field.
questions.correctAnswer: It allows current to flow much more easily in one direction than the other.
questions.explanation: A diode has a low resistance in the forward direction and a very high resistance in the reverse direction.

distractorRationales.text: It has the same resistance in both directions.
distractorRationales.misconception: diode treated as ohmic conductor

distractorRationales.text: It stores charge like a cell.
distractorRationales.misconception: diode confused with energy source

distractorRationales.text: It changes alternating current into a magnetic field.
distractorRationales.misconception: component purpose confused

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the current-voltage characteristic of a diode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret reverse-bias behaviour for a diode.

questions.skill: Interpret reverse-bias behaviour for a diode.
questions.stem: A diode is connected in reverse bias. What current would you expect?
questions.question: A diode is connected in reverse bias. What current would you expect?
options: A very small current, close to zero.
A larger current than in forward bias.
The same current as a fixed resistor.
A current that increases linearly with negative potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: A very small current, close to zero.
questions.explanation: In reverse bias a diode has a very high resistance, so only a tiny current flows.

distractorRationales.text: A larger current than in forward bias.
distractorRationales.misconception: reverse bias confused with forward bias

distractorRationales.text: The same current as a fixed resistor.
distractorRationales.misconception: diode treated as ohmic conductor

distractorRationales.text: A current that increases linearly with negative potential difference.
distractorRationales.misconception: straight-line graph applied to diode

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret reverse-bias behaviour for a diode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Use data to decide whether a component is ohmic.

questions.skill: Use data to decide whether a component is ohmic.
questions.stem: A lamp has a current of 0.20 A at 2.0 V and 0.32 A at 4.0 V. What does this show?
questions.question: A lamp has a current of 0.20 A at 2.0 V and 0.32 A at 4.0 V. What does this show?
options: The lamp is not ohmic because current has not doubled.
The lamp is ohmic because potential difference has doubled.
The lamp has zero resistance at 4.0 V.
The lamp transfers no energy at 2.0 V.
questions.correctAnswer: The lamp is not ohmic because current has not doubled.
questions.explanation: If the lamp were ohmic, doubling the potential difference would double the current. The current rises by less, so resistance has increased.

distractorRationales.text: The lamp is ohmic because potential difference has doubled.
distractorRationales.misconception: only voltage compared

distractorRationales.text: The lamp has zero resistance at 4.0 V.
distractorRationales.misconception: non-linear graph confused with no resistance

distractorRationales.text: The lamp transfers no energy at 2.0 V.
distractorRationales.misconception: current flow confused with no energy transfer

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.15.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use data to decide whether a component is ohmic.

learningObjective.statement: Choose meter connections for an I-V practical.

questions.skill: Choose meter connections for an I-V practical.
questions.stem: In an I-V investigation, how should the meters be connected to test a resistor?
questions.question: In an I-V investigation, how should the meters be connected to test a resistor?
options: Ammeter in series; voltmeter in parallel with the resistor.

questions.skill: Analyse the risk when the earth wire is missing during a live-case fault.
questions.stem: A metal kettle has a damaged live wire touching its case, but the earth wire is broken. What is the main danger?
questions.question: A metal kettle has a damaged live wire touching its case, but the earth wire is broken. What is the main danger?
options: The metal case can remain live and give an electric shock.
The case becomes neutral and safe to touch.
The fuse must melt instantly without an earth path.
The kettle changes from a.c. to d.c.
questions.correctAnswer: The metal case can remain live and give an electric shock.
questions.explanation: Without a working earth wire, the metal case may stay live because there may not be a large enough current to melt the fuse.

distractorRationales.text: The case becomes neutral and safe to touch.
distractorRationales.misconception: fault risk underestimated

distractorRationales.text: The fuse must melt instantly without an earth path.
distractorRationales.misconception: earth path needed for large fault current

distractorRationales.text: The kettle changes from a.c. to d.c.
distractorRationales.misconception: supply type confused with fault

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.39.
questions.senNote: Checks: Analyse the risk when the earth wire is missing during a live-case fault.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the safety function of a circuit breaker.

questions.skill: Describe the safety function of a circuit breaker.
questions.stem: What is the safety role of a circuit breaker?
questions.question: What is the safety role of a circuit breaker?
options: It disconnects the circuit when the current becomes too large.
It increases the current when an appliance starts.
It stores electrical energy for later use.
It changes a.c. into d.c. for every appliance.
questions.correctAnswer: It disconnects the circuit when the current becomes too large.
questions.explanation: A circuit breaker opens the circuit when the current is too high, reducing the risk of overheating and shock.

distractorRationales.text: It increases the current when an appliance starts.
distractorRationales.misconception: breaker confused with supply booster

distractorRationales.text: It stores electrical energy for later use.
distractorRationales.misconception: breaker confused with battery

distractorRationales.text: It changes a.c. into d.c. for every appliance.
distractorRationales.misconception: breaker confused with converter

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.39.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the safety function of a circuit breaker.

learningObjective.statement: Relate appliance power rating to rate of energy transfer.

questions.skill: Relate appliance power rating to rate of energy transfer.
questions.stem: Two kettles are used for the same time. Kettle X has a power rating of 3.0 kW and kettle Y has a power rating of 1.5 kW. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: Two kettles are used for the same time. Kettle X has a power rating of 3.0 kW and kettle Y has a power rating of 1.5 kW. Which statement is correct?
options: Kettle X transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle Y.
Kettle Y transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle X.
Both kettles transfer energy at the same rate.
Power rating tells you the mass of the kettle.
questions.correctAnswer: Kettle X transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle Y.
questions.explanation: Power is the rate of energy transfer. 3.0 kW is twice 1.5 kW, so kettle X transfers energy twice as quickly.

distractorRationales.text: Kettle Y transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle X.
distractorRationales.misconception: power ratio reversed

distractorRationales.text: Both kettles transfer energy at the same rate.
distractorRationales.misconception: different ratings ignored

distractorRationales.text: Power rating tells you the mass of the kettle.
distractorRationales.misconception: power meaning confused

questions.question: A 690 W appliance is connected to the 230 V mains. What current does it normally take?
questions.explanation: Use P = V × I, so I = P ÷ V. I = 690 ÷ 230 = 3.0 A.

learningObjective.statement: Apply domestic electricity safety rules to damaged insulation.

questions.skill: Apply domestic electricity safety rules to damaged insulation.
questions.stem: A plug has cracked insulation so that bare copper from the live wire can be touched. What should be done?
questions.question: A plug has cracked insulation so that bare copper from the live wire can be touched. What should be done?
options: Stop using the appliance until the plug or cable is repaired.
Use the appliance only when the switch is off.
Replace the earth wire with a thicker live wire.
Wrap the fuse in foil so it cannot melt.
questions.correctAnswer: Stop using the appliance until the plug or cable is repaired.
questions.explanation: Damaged insulation can expose the live conductor and cause electric shock, so the appliance should not be used until repaired.

distractorRationales.text: Use the appliance only when the switch is off.
distractorRationales.misconception: live wire may still be dangerous

distractorRationales.text: Replace the earth wire with a thicker live wire.
distractorRationales.misconception: unsafe wiring change

distractorRationales.text: Wrap the fuse in foil so it cannot melt.
distractorRationales.misconception: bypassing fuse is dangerous

Charge is destroyed inside the heater.

Current is stored in the heating element.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is transferred electrically to the thermal store of the room.
questions.explanation: A heater transfers energy electrically. The useful output is an increase in thermal energy of the surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: Charge is destroyed inside the heater.
Potential difference is measured in joules.
Current is stored in the heating element.

learningObjective.statement: Compare energy transferred using power ratings.

questions.skill: Compare energy transferred using power ratings.
questions.stem: A 1 kW heater and a 2 kW heater are each used for 30 minutes. Which heater transfers more energy?
questions.question: A 1 kW heater and a 2 kW heater are each used for 30 minutes. Which heater transfers more energy?
options: The 1 kW heater, because it has the lower power.
The 2 kW heater, because it transfers energy at a greater rate.
Both heaters transfer the same energy because the time is the same.
Neither heater transfers energy unless the time is measured in seconds.
questions.correctAnswer: The 2 kW heater, because it transfers energy at a greater rate.
questions.explanation: For the same time, the appliance with the greater power transfers more energy.

distractorRationales.text: The 1 kW heater, because it has the lower power.
Both heaters transfer the same energy because the time is the same.
Neither heater transfers energy unless the time is measured in seconds.

learningObjective.statement: Explain wasted energy in an electrical device.

questions.skill: Explain wasted energy in an electrical device.
questions.stem: A phone charger transfers 18000 J from the mains. The phone battery gains 12600 J. What has happened to the remaining energy?
questions.question: A phone charger transfers 18000 J from the mains. The phone battery gains 12600 J. What has happened to the remaining energy?
options: It has been wasted, mostly by heating the charger and surroundings.
It has disappeared from the circuit.
It has turned into extra charge.
It has become potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: It has been wasted, mostly by heating the charger and surroundings.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved. The remaining energy is transferred to less useful stores, mainly heating the charger and surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: It has disappeared from the circuit.
It has turned into extra charge.
It has become potential difference.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.29.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain wasted energy in an electrical device.

It does not need a complete circuit.

distractorRationales.text: It has no current through it.
It does not need a complete circuit.
It makes the electricity meter run backwards.

The resistance of a 1 kW appliance.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why lower-power appliances use less energy for the same time.

questions.skill: Explain why lower-power appliances use less energy for the same time.
questions.stem: Why does replacing a 60 W filament lamp with a 9 W LED reduce energy use for the same time switched on?
questions.question: Why does replacing a 60 W filament lamp with a 9 W LED reduce energy use for the same time switched on?
options: The LED transfers energy at a lower rate.
The LED has no current through it.
The filament lamp has zero resistance.
The LED makes electricity instead of using it.
questions.correctAnswer: The LED transfers energy at a lower rate.
questions.explanation: A lower power rating means less energy is transferred each second, so less energy is used over the same time.

distractorRationales.text: The LED has no current through it.
The filament lamp has zero resistance.
The LED makes electricity instead of using it.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why lower-power appliances use less energy for the same time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate maximum power from supply limits.

questions.skill: Calculate maximum power from supply limits.
questions.stem: A power supply can safely provide a maximum current of 2 A at 12 V. What is the maximum power it can provide?
questions.question: A power supply can safely provide a maximum current of 2 A at 12 V. What is the maximum power it can provide?
options: 6 W
questions.explanation: Power = potential difference x current, so P = 12 x 2 = 24 W.

distractorRationales.text: 6 W

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate maximum power from supply limits.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Use a power-time graph to calculate energy transferred.

questions.skill: Use a power-time graph to calculate energy transferred.
questions.stem: A power-time graph is horizontal at 40 W for 5 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A power-time graph is horizontal at 40 W for 5 s. How much energy is transferred?
options: 8 J
questions.explanation: Energy transferred is the area under a power-time graph. E = 40 x 5 = 200 J.

distractorRationales.text: 8 J

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use a power-time graph to calculate energy transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the heating effect of a current in a resistor.

questions.skill: Describe the heating effect of a current in a resistor.
questions.stem: A resistor gets warmer when a current passes through it. What energy transfer is taking place?
questions.question: A resistor gets warmer when a current passes through it. What energy transfer is taking place?
options: Electrical energy is transferred to the thermal store of the resistor.
Thermal energy is transferred to the electrical store of the battery.
Nuclear energy is transferred to the chemical store of the resistor.
Kinetic energy is transferred to gravitational potential energy only.
questions.correctAnswer: Electrical energy is transferred to the thermal store of the resistor.
questions.explanation: Current through a resistor transfers electrical energy to the thermal store of the resistor and its surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: Thermal energy is transferred to the electrical store of the battery.
Nuclear energy is transferred to the chemical store of the resistor.
Kinetic energy is transferred to gravitational potential energy only.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the heating effect of a current in a resistor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare energy transfer using power and time.

questions.skill: Compare energy transfer using power and time.
questions.stem: A 100 W device and a 200 W device are each used for 10 s. Which device transfers more energy?
questions.question: A 100 W device and a 200 W device are each used for 10 s. Which device transfers more energy?
options: The 100 W device, because it has the lower power.
The 200 W device, because it transfers energy twice as fast.
Both transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
Neither transfers energy unless the time is measured in hours.
questions.correctAnswer: The 200 W device, because it transfers energy twice as fast.
questions.explanation: For the same time, the device with twice the power transfers twice as much energy.

distractorRationales.text: The 100 W device, because it has the lower power.
Both transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
Neither transfers energy unless the time is measured in hours.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare energy transfer using power and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Explain an overload risk using current and power.

questions.skill: Explain an overload risk using current and power.
questions.stem: Why can using too many high-power appliances on one extension lead be dangerous?
questions.question: Why can using too many high-power appliances on one extension lead be dangerous?
options: The total current can become too large and overheat the lead.
The appliances stop using energy.
The mains potential difference falls to zero instantly.
The lead becomes a perfect insulator.
questions.correctAnswer: The total current can become too large and overheat the lead.
questions.explanation: High-power appliances can draw large currents. If the total current is too large, wires can overheat.

distractorRationales.text: The appliances stop using energy.
The mains potential difference falls to zero instantly.
The lead becomes a perfect insulator.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.31.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain an overload risk using current and power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the structure of a parallel circuit.

questions.skill: Describe the structure of a parallel circuit.
questions.stem: Which circuit has components connected in parallel?
questions.question: Which circuit has components connected in parallel?
options: A circuit with more than one path for current.
A circuit with only one path for current.
A circuit with no source of potential difference.
A circuit where current cannot split at any point.
questions.correctAnswer: A circuit with more than one path for current.
questions.explanation: A parallel circuit has branches, giving current more than one path to follow.

distractorRationales.text: A circuit with only one path for current.
distractorRationales.misconception: series circuit confused with parallel

distractorRationales.text: A circuit with no source of potential difference.
distractorRationales.misconception: incomplete circuit confused with parallel

distractorRationales.text: A circuit where current cannot split at any point.
distractorRationales.misconception: junction behaviour misunderstood

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the structure of a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Recall that potential difference is the same across parallel branches.

questions.skill: Recall that potential difference is the same across parallel branches.
questions.stem: Two lamps are connected in parallel to a 6 V battery. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected in parallel to a 6 V battery. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
options: 6 V across each lamp
3 V across each lamp
12 V across each lamp
0 V across each lamp
questions.correctAnswer: 6 V across each lamp
questions.explanation: Components in parallel are connected across the same two points, so each branch has the full supply potential difference.

distractorRationales.text: 3 V across each lamp
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel potential difference confused with series sharing

distractorRationales.text: 12 V across each lamp
distractorRationales.misconception: supply voltage incorrectly added

distractorRationales.text: 0 V across each lamp
distractorRationales.misconception: complete circuit misunderstood

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall that potential difference is the same across parallel branches.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Use conservation of current at a junction.

questions.skill: Use conservation of current at a junction.
questions.stem: A current of 0.60 A reaches a junction. One branch has a current of 0.20 A. What is the current in the other branch?
questions.question: A current of 0.60 A reaches a junction. One branch has a current of 0.20 A. What is the current in the other branch?
questions.explanation: Current is conserved at a junction. The branch currents add to the total, so the other branch is 0.60 - 0.20 = 0.40 A.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use conservation of current at a junction.

questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate total current in a parallel circuit.

questions.skill: Calculate total current in a parallel circuit.
questions.stem: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.30 A and 0.45 A. What is the total current from the cell?
questions.question: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.30 A and 0.45 A. What is the total current from the cell?
questions.explanation: The total current is the sum of the branch currents: 0.30 + 0.45 = 0.75 A.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate total current in a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe how adding a parallel resistor affects total resistance.

questions.skill: Describe how adding a parallel resistor affects total resistance.
questions.stem: A second identical resistor is added in parallel with the first. What happens to the total resistance of the circuit?
questions.question: A second identical resistor is added in parallel with the first. What happens to the total resistance of the circuit?
options: It decreases.
It doubles.
It stays the same.
It becomes infinite.
questions.correctAnswer: It decreases.
questions.explanation: Adding a parallel branch gives charge another path, so the total resistance decreases.

distractorRationales.text: It doubles.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel resistors confused with series resistors

distractorRationales.text: It stays the same.
distractorRationales.misconception: extra branch ignored

distractorRationales.text: It becomes infinite.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel path confused with broken circuit

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe how adding a parallel resistor affects total resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Predict the effect of a broken branch in a parallel circuit.

questions.skill: Predict the effect of a broken branch in a parallel circuit.
questions.stem: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One lamp breaks. What happens to the other lamp?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One lamp breaks. What happens to the other lamp?
options: It stays lit if its branch is still complete.
It must go out because all current used the broken lamp first.
It becomes twice as bright in every circuit.
It changes into a resistor with no current.
questions.correctAnswer: It stays lit if its branch is still complete.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch has its own path. A break in one branch does not necessarily break the other branch.

distractorRationales.text: It must go out because all current used the broken lamp first.
distractorRationales.misconception: series behaviour applied to parallel circuit

distractorRationales.text: It becomes twice as bright in every circuit.
distractorRationales.misconception: brightness overgeneralised

distractorRationales.text: It changes into a resistor with no current.
distractorRationales.misconception: component identity confused

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Predict the effect of a broken branch in a parallel circuit.

questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare the structure of series and parallel circuits.

questions.skill: Compare the structure of series and parallel circuits.
questions.stem: Which statement correctly compares series and parallel circuits?
questions.question: Which statement correctly compares series and parallel circuits?
options: Series circuits have one path; parallel circuits have more than one path.
Series circuits always have a larger supply voltage.
Parallel circuits have no junctions.
Parallel circuits cannot contain lamps.
questions.correctAnswer: Series circuits have one path; parallel circuits have more than one path.
questions.explanation: A series circuit has a single loop. A parallel circuit has branches and junctions.

distractorRationales.text: Series circuits always have a larger supply voltage.
distractorRationales.misconception: supply voltage confused with layout

distractorRationales.text: Parallel circuits have no junctions.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel branch structure misunderstood

distractorRationales.text: Parallel circuits cannot contain lamps.
distractorRationales.misconception: component type confused with circuit type

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for Edexcel 10.3/10.11/10.14/10.17.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare the structure of series and parallel circuits.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Relate branch resistance to branch current in parallel.

questions.skill: Relate branch resistance to branch current in parallel.
questions.stem: Two parallel branches are connected to the same battery. Branch X has a lower resistance than branch Y. Which branch has the larger current?
questions.question: Two parallel branches are connected to the same battery. Branch X has a lower resistance than branch Y. Which branch has the larger current?
options: Branch X
Branch Y
Both branches must have zero current.
Both branches must have exactly the same current.
questions.correctAnswer: Branch X
questions.explanation: The potential difference is the same across each branch. The branch with lower resistance has the larger current.

distractorRationales.text: Branch Y
distractorRationales.misconception: higher resistance incorrectly linked to larger current

distractorRationales.text: Both branches must have zero current.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel circuit mistaken for open circuit

distractorRationales.text: Both branches must have exactly the same current.
distractorRationales.misconception: same potential difference confused with same current

learningObjective.statement: Choose where to connect an ammeter in a parallel branch.

questions.skill: Choose where to connect an ammeter in a parallel branch.
questions.stem: Where should an ammeter be placed to measure the current in one branch of a parallel circuit?
questions.question: Where should an ammeter be placed to measure the current in one branch of a parallel circuit?
options: In series with the component in that branch.
In parallel across the whole battery.
Across the switch only.
Between two points without completing a path.
questions.correctAnswer: In series with the component in that branch.
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures the current through a branch, so it must be connected in series within that branch.

distractorRationales.text: In parallel across the whole battery.
distractorRationales.misconception: voltmeter connection confused with ammeter

distractorRationales.text: Across the switch only.
distractorRationales.misconception: switch measurement confused with branch current

distractorRationales.text: Between two points without completing a path.
distractorRationales.misconception: ammeter must be part of circuit path

learningObjective.statement: Choose where to connect a voltmeter in a parallel circuit.

questions.skill: Choose where to connect a voltmeter in a parallel circuit.
questions.stem: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across one branch?
questions.question: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across one branch?
options: In parallel with that branch.
In series before the junction.
Only on the live side of the battery.
In place of the component.

questions.explanation: A voltmeter compares two points, so it is connected in parallel across the branch or component.

distractorRationales.misconception: one-point measurement misconception

distractorRationales.text: In place of the component.
distractorRationales.misconception: meter replacing component rather than measuring across it

questions.stem: In a practical, a student tests two resistors in parallel. Which measurement would show that the branches have the same potential difference?
questions.question: In a practical, a student tests two resistors in parallel. Which measurement would show that the branches have the same potential difference?
options: A voltmeter reading across each branch.
An ammeter reading before the first branch only.
A thermometer reading on each resistor.
A ruler measurement of the wire length.

learningObjective.statement: Apply current conservation to a three-branch parallel circuit.

questions.skill: Apply current conservation to a three-branch parallel circuit.
questions.stem: A current of 1.2 A leaves a cell and splits into three branches. Two branch currents are 0.50 A and 0.30 A. What is the third branch current?
questions.question: A current of 1.2 A leaves a cell and splits into three branches. Two branch currents are 0.50 A and 0.30 A. What is the third branch current?
questions.explanation: The branch currents must add to 1.2 A. The missing current is 1.2 - 0.50 - 0.30 = 0.40 A.

questions.skill: Predict total current when another parallel branch is added.
questions.stem: A third lamp is added in parallel to two lamps already connected to a battery. What happens to the total current from the battery?
questions.question: A third lamp is added in parallel to two lamps already connected to a battery. What happens to the total current from the battery?

It decreases to zero.

distractorRationales.text: It must stay the same.

learningObjective.statement: Compare total parallel resistance with individual branch resistance.

questions.skill: Compare total parallel resistance with individual branch resistance.
questions.stem: Two 10 ohm resistors are connected in parallel. How does their total resistance compare with 10 ohms?
questions.question: Two 10 ohm resistors are connected in parallel. How does their total resistance compare with 10 ohms?
options: It is less than 10 ohms.
It is exactly 10 ohms.
It is 20 ohms.
It is impossible for current to flow.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less than 10 ohms.

learningObjective.statement: Apply parallel circuit ideas to domestic lighting.

questions.skill: Apply parallel circuit ideas to domestic lighting.
questions.stem: Why are lamps in a house normally connected in parallel rather than all in series?
questions.question: Why are lamps in a house normally connected in parallel rather than all in series?
options: Each lamp can receive the mains potential difference and work independently.
A series circuit gives every lamp the full mains potential difference.
Parallel circuits stop all current from flowing.
A broken lamp in parallel always switches off every other lamp.
questions.correctAnswer: Each lamp can receive the mains potential difference and work independently.
questions.explanation: In parallel, each lamp has the supply potential difference and one branch can be switched without breaking every other branch.

distractorRationales.misconception: series voltage sharing misunderstood

distractorRationales.text: Parallel circuits stop all current from flowing.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel path misunderstood

distractorRationales.text: A broken lamp in parallel always switches off every other lamp.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel independence reversed

The readings are impossible because every branch must have 0.90 A.

distractorRationales.misconception: total current confused with branch current

distractorRationales.text: The circuit must be series because there are three ammeters.
distractorRationales.misconception: equipment count confused with circuit layout

distractorRationales.text: The battery has no potential difference.
distractorRationales.misconception: current readings ignored
